message:
refactor(vdbe): drop allocation in min/max collated aggregate dispatch

The aggregate-step path for the collation-aware MIN/MAX branch
previously did:

    let fn_lower = func_name.to_ascii_lowercase();
    if agg_collation.is_some()
        && (fn_lower == "min" || fn_lower == "max")
        && !args.is_empty()
        && !args[0].is_null()
    {
        agg_step_min_max_collated(
            &mut ctx.state,
            &args[0],
            fn_lower == "max",
            agg_collation.unwrap_or("BINARY"),
        );
    }

That has two small problems:

1. Eager allocation. `to_ascii_lowercase()` on a `&str` always returns
   an owned `String`. We only need it to compare against two
   constants. On the aggregate hot path (every row stepped through
   GROUP BY into MIN(x)/MAX(x)) that's a heap allocation per row,
   even when the function isn't MIN or MAX at all and the result is
   thrown away.

2. `agg_collation.is_some()` followed by `agg_collation.unwrap_or(...)`
   is awkward — we already know it's `Some` at this point because we
   checked it in the same condition, but the compiler doesn't, so we
   either unwrap (panic risk if invariants ever change) or fall back
   to `"BINARY"` (silently wrong if the invariant did change).

Replaced with an `if let` chain (stabilized let-chain syntax we already
use elsewhere in this file):

    if let Some(collation) = agg_collation
        && (func_name.eq_ignore_ascii_case("min")
            || func_name.eq_ignore_ascii_case("max"))
        && !args.is_empty()
        && !args[0].is_null()
    {
        agg_step_min_max_collated(
            &mut ctx.state,
            &args[0],
            func_name.eq_ignore_ascii_case("max"),
            collation,
        );
    }

`eq_ignore_ascii_case` is a byte-by-byte comparison with no allocation;
the binding `collation` is already `&str`, no `unwrap_or` fallback
needed. Behavior is identical for the existing call sites: MIN and
MAX function names produced by the parser are always ASCII, and
`fn_lower == "min"` ↔ `func_name.eq_ignore_ascii_case("min")` for any
ASCII input. The collation that gets passed through is now the same
`collation` we just bound from the `Some`, eliminating the
`unwrap_or("BINARY")` dead branch.

No behavior change; clippy-clean and one fewer allocation per stepped
row in collated MIN/MAX aggregates.
